instrument.title: EU AI Act enforcement
instrument.thesis: What is actually enforced versus what was announced. Tracking how far Europe has stood up the law it wrote, and what has happened to anyone who broke it.
instrument.refresh_note: Updated weekly · last refresh 3 Jun 2026, 06:00 CET

what_changed: The EU agreed to push two more sets of high-risk obligations to 2027 and 2028, before either took effect. Fines issued stayed at zero.

forward_call.this_week_expects: The Omnibus to be published in the Official Journal before the August deadline.
forward_call._note: prior_call and prior_call_grade are null for Edition 01. From Edition 02, prior_call carries last week's expectation and prior_call_grade is 'correct' or 'wrong'.

map.title: Who has actually named their enforcers
map.subtitle: National competent authorities, designation status. Deadline was 2 August 2025.
map.helperText: Tap a country for its designation status.

gap_clock.anchor_label: days prohibited practices have been in force
gap_clock._note: days-in-force is computed at render from anchor_date to today, so it ticks without edits. fines_issued is maintained by hand.

body: The European Union wrote the world's first comprehensive law for artificial intelligence, and it has spent the months since easing its own grip on it. Under the Digital Omnibus agreed in early May, the obligations for high-risk systems are set to slide from this August to December 2027, and for AI built into regulated products to 2028. They will have been postponed before a single one took effect, and the amendment that postpones them is itself not yet final.

What is already binding has been binding for a while. Prohibited practices have been enforceable across all twenty-seven member states since February 2025, the rules for general-purpose models since last August. And yet no fine has been issued anywhere under the Act, and only a handful of member states have formally named the authorities meant to enforce it, nearly a year past the deadline to do so.

This is the gap yellow3 tracks here each week: the distance between a law that exists and the machinery to apply it, which does not. Europe has proven it can legislate AI faster than anyone. Whether it can enforce what it wrote is the more interesting question, and the one the next two years will answer. The first thing to watch is narrow and soon: whether the Omnibus reaches the Official Journal before August, making the delay it promises real.

methodology.note: Designation status is yellow3's reading of the European Commission's consolidated list of notified authorities, cross-checked against the IAPP regulatory directory and the Future of Life Institute implementation timeline, as of the stated date. In-force dates reflect the AI Act as amended by the Digital Omnibus where provisionally agreed, and may shift until formal publication in the Official Journal. This tracks legal status and public enforcement actions, not compliance behaviour inside companies.
methodology.source_line: Sources: European Commission AI Act Service Desk; artificialintelligenceact.eu (Future of Life Institute). As of 3 Jun 2026
